preFix.title: Growing fodder crops for meat rabbits
preFix.subtitle: Cut feed costs and improve health by growing what rabbits evolved to eat.
preFix.excerpt: Commercial pellets are convenient but expensive at scale. Rabbits thrive on a diet with fresh greens alongside hay and pellets, and most smallholders with any outdoor space can grow enough to meaningfully reduce the feed bill. A guide to the crops that grow fast, the safe plants, and how to introduce fresh fodder without digestive upset.
preFix.sourceNotes: University of Wisconsin Extension, small-scale rabbit production (open-access). Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), chapter on rabbits, Project Gutenberg. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits, Crown copyright.

content.text: In spring and summer, a small patch of ground can supply meaningful quantities of fresh fodder. Rabbits are hindgut fermenters and digest fibre well; they do less well on too many pellets and not enough variety. Fresh greens supply water, vitamins, and the phytonutrients that a pellet-only diet lacks.

Kale and cavolo nero are the most practical crops. They grow quickly from seed, stand in the ground from autumn through spring, and rabbits eat them with enthusiasm. Sow in March or April for autumn cutting, or in September for a spring crop. Cut outer leaves and leave the plant to re-grow.

Comfrey (Symphytum officinale) is a deep-rooted perennial that produces enormous quantities of leaf through the growing season. Plant root cuttings in spring; cut at 30 cm height up to four times per season. A 2-metre row yields enough for a colony of four rabbits as a supplement throughout summer.

Grass, dandelion, plantain, clover, and chicory are all eaten. If you have a lawn, mow strips and collect the clippings. Let fresh clippings wilt for half an hour before feeding; wet clippings can cause loose stools. Avoid clippings that have been cut by a petrol mower with oil residue on the blades.

attrs.body: Do not feed rhubarb leaves, foxglove, buttercup, nightshade, or bulb plants such as daffodil. These are toxic to rabbits.
attrs.title: Plants to avoid

content.text: The rabbit gut takes time to adjust to new foods. Introducing a large amount of fresh greens to a rabbit raised entirely on pellets and hay can cause

. Start with a small handful per adult rabbit per day, one new crop at a time. Watch for loose stools or signs of discomfort in the first 48 hours. If you see either, withdraw the new food and return to hay and pellets for a week before trying again.
Once rabbits are established on fresh fodder, their
generally improves, particularly in adults. Never replace hay entirely. Hay must be available at all times; it supplies the long-strand fibre that keeps the gut moving.

Cut in the morning once the dew has dried. Wet leaves left in the hutch in a pile will ferment and cause disease. Spread the cut fodder loosely in the hutch or hang it from the wire in small bunches so it dries further before the rabbits eat it.

In winter, when fresh crops are finished, dried herbs make a useful supplement. Hang bunches of rosemary, sage, or dried nettle to dry in summer and store in paper sacks. Feed small quantities as a treat alongside the main hay ration.

A 4 m by 2 m bed divided into four strips gives a simple cut-and-come-again rotation. Sow one strip to kale, one to comfrey (permanent), one to mixed salad greens, and leave the fourth for the rabbits to graze directly if you run a
system with portable netting. Rotate the access around the bed and let each strip rest and re-grow between grazings. Water the bed during dry spells to keep production up.

items.fix: Remove fresh greens immediately. Return to hay and dry pellets for a week. Reintroduce one crop at a time starting with grass or dried herbs.
items.cause: Too much leafy green introduced too fast
items.symptom: Rabbit producing very soft or liquid caecotropes after fresh greens

items.fix: Offer only fresh-cut or very recently wilted greens. Try different crops. Some animals take several days before accepting a new food source.
items.cause: Unfamiliar food, or fodder that has wilted and fermented

items.fix: Limit direct-access grazing to 20 minutes per session. Use portable netting to rotate access rather than leaving the rabbits on the bed all day.
items.cause: Overgrazing; rabbit grazed the crop too hard before it could re-grow
items.symptom: Kale plants eaten to the stem by rabbits given direct access
